Well, first of all, we call it a funnel because it looks like one as you can see here. Essentially, what a marketing funnel is, is it's a way to attract leads into your business and then take them through a marketing sequence to build trust and affinity so that they do business with you. This funnel sometimes going to start off targeting people that have never heard of you or your product or your service before. And other funnels are going to target existing customers to build sort of loyalty and affinity and to create repeat customers. So if you look at this within a funnel, your landing page is going to be your first step in your funnel is going to be your opt-in page, sometimes referred to as a squeeps page, but it's a bit of an aggressive term and I'm not very aggressive in nature. So I prefer to use the term opt-in page. So we'll just go with that for now. And your opt-in page is essentially the entry point of your funnel. You can use that in a million ways. There's no limit to what you can do with your opt-in page, but we'll have a little look at that in a second. Generally, what you want to use your opt-in page for is to collect email addresses or collect data, really, which can later be used for your nurture sequences. For now, here's some basics that you need to know about your opt-in page. So it's always the first step of the funnel. It's your entry point. People click your ad and this is the page they see. Your opt-in page needs to be a clear continuation of your ad. If, say, your messaging or what you're saying on your opt-in page is completely different than what you were saying in your ad copy. For example, if you presented a problem in your ad copy and it doesn't reflect that on the opt-in page, what you're doing is what we call losing the scent.

## [06:11 - 09:13]

And that means when there's a disconnect there, people aren't really going to click through on that opt-in page. So a bright, clear call to action above the fold as well. So you want people to see that button before they scroll. You need to tell them clearly what you want them to do next. And then also what you want to avoid on your opt-in page is distractions. You do not want it to contain any distractions. And this is why your landing page can't be your website. I mean, you can send people to your website, but this is why I prefer not to use a website as a landing page because there's buttons, there's menu buttons, people can click away. Even if you're as you're going directly to your products, if you look in the back end of your Shopify, the About page is the most visited page. So people love to click away. People love to sort of get distracted. Don't give them a chance to. And that's sort of the beauty of the landing page. And that's why they convert so well is because there's nowhere else to click. It's literally like quite literally like a funnel. There's only one way down. Only give them the option to click what you want them to click. Moving on to your sales page. If you're pitching a high-priced product, you may want to leave that price pitching up to a salesperson. They were not aware of who you were, what your service was. Maybe they weren't even aware of what their problem was. So you just sent the cold audience to your opt-in page at your top of funnel. thank you email. Thank you that you booked a call. Our sales team will be in touch or it can be just a way to introduce yourself or your service.
